In 50% solution moles of H2SO4=0.01 m moles

Added moles of H2SO4=0.01 m moles

Total moles of H2SO4 in resulting solution =0.02 m moles

=20×10-3 m moles

=20×10-3 milimoles

subject: Chemistry
q:
A metal chloride contains 55.0% of chlorine by weight. 100 mL vapours of the metal chloride at STP weigh 0.57 g. The molecular formula of the metal chloride is

(Given: Atomic mass of chlorine is 35.5 u)
question:
A metal chloride contains 55.0% of chlorine by weight. 100 mL vapours of the metal chloride at STP weigh 0.57 g. The molecular formula of the metal chloride is

(Given: Atomic mass of chlorine is 35.5 u)
solution:
Let the formula of metal chloride be MClx

Mass of 100 mL of vapours of MClx at STP =0.57 g

The mass of substance which occupies 22400 ml of volume at STP is molar mass(M)

∴ 100×M22400=0.57

∴ Molar mass of MClx=127.68 g mol–1

The mass percentage of chlorine=mass of chlorinemass of the compound×100

% of Cl=35.5x×100127.68=55

⇒ x=2

∴ Formula of metal chloride is MCl2

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of mass percent of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> of an organic compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If one molecule of the above compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> contains half as much oxygen as required to burn one molecule of compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></msub></math> completely to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The empirical formula of the compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></math> is
question: The ratio of mass percent of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><mi>and</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></math> of an organic compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> If one molecule of the above compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> contains half as much oxygen as required to burn one molecule of compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></msub></math> completely to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The empirical formula of the compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Z</mi></msub></math> is
solution:
Mass of C: Mass of H=6 : 1.
Or, for every atom of C (12 u), we have 2 atoms of H (1u × 2=2u)
Thus, the ratio of atoms of C : H=x : y=1 : 2
Thus, the formula is: CxH2xOz
Combustion of CxH2x:
CxH2x+(3x/2)O2→xCO2+xH2O
Hence, the combustion of CxH2x requires (3x/2) oxygen molecules.

Hence, the original molecule contains (3x/4) molecules =(3x/2) atoms.
Or, z=3x/2

Empirical Formula=CxH2xO3x/2=C2xH4xO3x=C2H4O3

subject: Chemistry
q:
At 300 K and 1 atm, 15 mL of a gaseous hydrocarbon requires 375 mL air containing 20% O2 by volume, for complete combustion. After combustion, the gases occupy 345 mL. Assuming that the water formed is in liquid form and the volumes were measured at the same temperature and pressure, the formula of the hydrocarbon is:
(Assume complete combustion of reactant)
question:
At 300 K and 1 atm, 15 mL of a gaseous hydrocarbon requires 375 mL air containing 20% O2 by volume, for complete combustion. After combustion, the gases occupy 345 mL. Assuming that the water formed is in liquid form and the volumes were measured at the same temperature and pressure, the formula of the hydrocarbon is:
(Assume complete combustion of reactant)
solution:
Volume of N2 in air=375×0.8=300 ml
Volume of O2 in air=375×0.2=75 ml
C x H y + x + y 4 O 2 ⟶ xCO 2 g + y 2 H 2 O ℓ 1 5 ml 1 5 x + y 4 0 0 1 5 x -
After combustion, total volume
345=VN2+VCO2
345=300+15x
x=3
Volume of O2 used
15x+y4=75
x+y4=5
y=8
So, hydrocarbon isC3H8.

subject: Chemistry
q: $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $2(\mathrm{M}) \mathrm{~NaOH}$ solution is added to $200 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.5$ $\mathrm{(M)}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution. What is the final concentration ?
question: $10 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $2(\mathrm{M}) \mathrm{~NaOH}$ solution is added to $200 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $0.5$ $\mathrm{(M)}$ of $\mathrm{NaOH}$ solution. What is the final concentration ?
solution:
From molarity equation
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{M}_1 \mathrm{V}_1+\mathrm{M}_2 \mathrm{V}_2=\mathrm{MV}_{\text {(total) }} \\
& 2 \times \frac{10}{1000}+0.5 \times \frac{200}{1000}=\mathrm{M} \times \frac{210}{1000} \\
& 120=\mathrm{M} \times 210 \\
& \mathrm{M}=\frac{120}{210}=0.57 \mathrm{~M}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A sample of $\mathrm{NaClO}_3$ is converted by heat to $\mathrm{NaCl}$ with a loss of $0.16 \mathrm{~g}$ of oxygen. The residue is dissolved in water and precipitated as $\mathrm{AgCl}$. The mass of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ (in $\mathrm{g}$) obtained will be:
(Given: Molar mass of $\mathrm{AgCl}=143.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
question:
A sample of $\mathrm{NaClO}_3$ is converted by heat to $\mathrm{NaCl}$ with a loss of $0.16 \mathrm{~g}$ of oxygen. The residue is dissolved in water and precipitated as $\mathrm{AgCl}$. The mass of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ (in $\mathrm{g}$) obtained will be:
(Given: Molar mass of $\mathrm{AgCl}=143.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
solution:
No. of moles of oxygen in $0.16 \mathrm{~g}$ of oxygen molecule
$=\frac{0.16 \mathrm{~g}}{32 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}}=0.005 \mathrm{~mole}$
$2 \mathrm{NaClO}_3 \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} 2 \mathrm{NaCl}+3 \mathrm{O}_2$
According to the reaction, 
$3$ moles of $\mathrm{O}_2=2$ moles of $\mathrm{NaCl}$ 
$=2$ moles of $\mathrm{AgCl}$
Molar mass of $\mathrm{AgCl}=143.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}$
$0.005$ moles of $\mathrm{O}_2$ will ppt. $=0.005 \times \frac{2}{3}$ moles $\mathrm{AgCl}$
$=0.0033 \text { moles of } \mathrm{AgCl}$
$\therefore$ Mass of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ (in $\mathrm{g}$) obtained will be 
$=143.5 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol} \times 0.0033$ moles $=0.48 \mathrm{~g}$.

q:
If a rocket runs on a fuel C15H30 and liquid oxygen, the weight of oxygen required and CO2 released for every litre of fuel respectively are :
(Given : density of the fuel is 0.756 g/mL)
question:
If a rocket runs on a fuel C15H30 and liquid oxygen, the weight of oxygen required and CO2 released for every litre of fuel respectively are :
(Given : density of the fuel is 0.756 g/mL)
solution:
density=massvolume

mass of C15H30=d×V=0.756×1000=756 gram

C15H30l+452O2g⟶15CO2g+15H2O

Moles of C15H30 = 756210 Moles of O2 used= 452756210 mole  Moles of CO2 formed=15756210

WO2=45275621032=2592 gram

WCO2=2376 gram

subject: Chemistry
q: How many litres of water must be added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> litre of aqueous solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> with a pH of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> to create an aqueous solution with pH of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>?
question: How many litres of water must be added to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> litre of aqueous solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>HCl</mi></math> with a pH of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> to create an aqueous solution with pH of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math>?
solution:
pH=1=-log [H+]

[H+]=10-1 M

Final pH=2; [H+]=10-2 M

M1V1=M2V2

(0.1 M× 1 Litre)=(0.01) V2

V2=10 L

Final volume = 10 L

Water added =10-1=9 L.

subject: Chemistry
q:
The minimum amount of O2g consumed per gram of reactant is for the reaction:

(Given atomic mass: Fe=56, O=16, Mg=24, P=31, C=12,H=1 )
question:
The minimum amount of O2g consumed per gram of reactant is for the reaction:

(Given atomic mass: Fe=56, O=16, Mg=24, P=31, C=12,H=1 )
options: <math><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>→</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
(i) 5 mole O2 required = 1 mole of P4 =124 gm P4

for per gram, O2  required = 5124 mole 

(ii) 1 mole O2 required = 2 mole Mg = 2×24 gm

for per gram, O2  required =148 mole

(iii) 3 mole O2 required = 2 mole Fe = 4×56 gm

for per gram Fe, O2 required =3112×2 mole

(iv) 5 mole O2 required - =1 mole C3H8 =44 gm C3H8

per gram C3H8, O2 required =544 mole

q:

In the above reaction, 5 g of toluene is converted into benzaldehyde with 92% yield. The amount of benzaldehyde produced is____ ×10-2 g
question:
​​​​​​​

In the above reaction, 5 g of toluene is converted into benzaldehyde with 92% yield. The amount of benzaldehyde produced is____ ×10-2 g
solution:
Molar mass of benzaldehyde=106 g/mol

Number of moles of toluene =592

Number of moles of Benzaldehyde =0.92×592

Mass of Benzaldehyde =0.92×592×106=5.30 g=530×10-2 g

subject: Chemistry
q: The density of ' \(x\) ' \(\mathrm{M}\) solution (' \(X\) ' molar) of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) is \(1.12 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\), while in molality, the concentration of the solution is \(3 \mathrm{~m}(3 \mathrm{molal})\). Then \(x\) is (Given : Molar mass of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) is \(40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\) )
question: The density of ' \(x\) ' \(\mathrm{M}\) solution (' \(X\) ' molar) of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) is \(1.12 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\), while in molality, the concentration of the solution is \(3 \mathrm{~m}(3 \mathrm{molal})\). Then \(x\) is (Given : Molar mass of \(\mathrm{NaOH}\) is \(40 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{mol}\) )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Molality }=\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{M}}{1000 \times \mathrm{d}-\mathrm{M} \times(\mathrm{Mw})_{\text {solute }}} \\ & 3=\frac{1000 \times \mathrm{x}}{1000 \times 1.12-(\mathrm{x} \times 40)} \\ & \mathrm{x}=3\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of number of oxygen atoms $(\mathrm{O})$ in $16.0 \mathrm{~g}$ ozone $\left(\mathrm{O}_3\right), 28.0 \mathrm{~g}$ carbon monoxide $(\mathrm{CO})$ and $16.0$ oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$ is (Atomic mass : $\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{O}=16$ and Avogadro's constant $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.0 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
question: The ratio of number of oxygen atoms $(\mathrm{O})$ in $16.0 \mathrm{~g}$ ozone $\left(\mathrm{O}_3\right), 28.0 \mathrm{~g}$ carbon monoxide $(\mathrm{CO})$ and $16.0$ oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$ is (Atomic mass : $\mathrm{C}=12, \mathrm{O}=16$ and Avogadro's constant $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=6.0 \times 10^{23} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ )
solution:
$16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_3=\frac{16}{48}$ mole
$$
\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{16}{48} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules } \\
& =3 \times \frac{16}{48} \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& =6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& 28.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CO}=\frac{28}{28} \text { mole }=1 \text { mole } \\
& =1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { molecules } \\
& =1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& =6.023 \times 10^{23} \text { atoms } \\
& 16.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{O}_2=\frac{16}{16} \text { mole }=1 \mathrm{~mole}
\end{aligned}
$$
$=1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ molecules
$=1 \times 6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
$=6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atoms
Therefore, the ratio is
$6.023 \times 10^{23}: 6.023 \times 10^{23}: 6.023 \times 10^{23}$
i.e. $1: 1: 1$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following contain the same number of atoms ? (Given : Molar mass in g mol$^{-1}$ of H, He, O and S are $1, 4, 16$ and $32$ respectively)
A. $2$ g of O$_2$ gas
B. $4$ g of SO$_2$ gas
C. $1400$ mL of O$_2$ at STP
D. $0.05$ L of He at STP
E. $0.0625$ mol of H$_2$ gas
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Which of the following contain the same number of atoms ? (Given : Molar mass in g mol$^{-1}$ of H, He, O and S are $1, 4, 16$ and $32$ respectively)
A. $2$ g of O$_2$ gas
B. $4$ g of SO$_2$ gas
C. $1400$ mL of O$_2$ at STP
D. $0.05$ L of He at STP
E. $0.0625$ mol of H$_2$ gas
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Number of atoms is calculated as the product of the number of moles, atomicity, and Avogadro's number ($N_A$).

For A: Moles of O$_2$ = $\dfrac{2}{32} = \dfrac{1}{16}$ mol. Atomicity of O$_2$ is $2$.
Number of atoms = $\dfrac{1}{16} \times 2 \times N_A = \dfrac{1}{8} N_A$

For B: Moles of SO$_2$ = $\dfrac{4}{64} = \dfrac{1}{16}$ mol. Atomicity of SO$_2$ is $3$.
Number of atoms = $\dfrac{1}{16} \times 3 \times N_A = \dfrac{3}{16} N_A$

For C: Moles of O$_2$ at STP = $\dfrac{1400}{22400} = \dfrac{1}{16}$ mol. Atomicity of O$_2$ is $2$.
Number of atoms = $\dfrac{1}{16} \times 2 \times N_A = \dfrac{1}{8} N_A$

For D: Moles of He at STP = $\dfrac{0.05}{22.4} = \dfrac{1}{448}$ mol. Atomicity of He is $1$.
Number of atoms = $\dfrac{1}{448} \times 1 \times N_A = \dfrac{1}{448} N_A$

For E: Moles of H$_2$ = $0.0625 = \dfrac{1}{16}$ mol. Atomicity of H$_2$ is $2$.
Number of atoms = $\dfrac{1}{16} \times 2 \times N_A = \dfrac{1}{8} N_A$

Comparing the number of atoms, A, C, and E contain the same number of atoms.

Answer: A, C and E only

subject: Chemistry
q: A solution of sugar is obtained by mixing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of its <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> solution and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo></math>of its <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>%</mo><mo> </mo></math>solution (both by mass). The mass percentage of the resulting sugar solution is _______ (Nearest integer)
question: A solution of sugar is obtained by mixing <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of its <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mo>%</mo></math> solution and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>500</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo></math>of its <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>%</mo><mo> </mo></math>solution (both by mass). The mass percentage of the resulting sugar solution is _______ (Nearest integer)
solution:
Mass of solution = 200 + 500 = 700 g

25% of sugar solution means 100g solution contain 25g sugar

40% of sugar solution means 100g solution contain 40g sugar

Mass of sugar = 0.25 × 200 + 0.40 × 500

= 50 + 200 = 250 g

Mass % of resulting solution

=250700×100=36%

q: Chlorophyll extracted from the crushed green leaves was dissolved in water to make <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math> of concentration $48 \mathrm{ppm}$. The number of atoms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math> in this solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>20</mn></msup></math> atoms. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Nearest Integer) (Given : Atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: Chlorophyll extracted from the crushed green leaves was dissolved in water to make <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> solution of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math> of concentration $48 \mathrm{ppm}$. The number of atoms of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math> in this solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>20</mn></msup></math> atoms. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is (Nearest Integer) (Given : Atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Mg</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>24</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Chlorophyll is the green pigment in plants, algae, and cyanobacteria that is essential for photosynthesis. Its central structure is an aromatic porphyrin ring system with a sequestered magnesium atom.

ppm=WMgVsol×106=48

⇒WMg=48×2×1000106

=48×2×10-3 g

nMg=WMg24=48×2×10-324

=4×10-3

Number of Mg atoms =4×10-3×6.02×1023=4×6.02×1020

=4×6.02×1020

=24.08×1020

∴ x=24.08

subject: Chemistry
q:
For the given reaction:
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
If $90 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is added to 300 mL of HCl which contains $38.55 \% \mathrm{HCl}$ by mass and has density $1.13 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$, then which of the following option is correct ?
Given molar mass of $\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Ca}$ and O are 1, 35.5, 40 and $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
question:
For the given reaction:
$\mathrm{CaCO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CaCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CO}_{2}$
If $90 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{CaCO}_{3}$ is added to 300 mL of HCl which contains $38.55 \% \mathrm{HCl}$ by mass and has density $1.13 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$, then which of the following option is correct ?
Given molar mass of $\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{Cl}, \mathrm{Ca}$ and O are 1, 35.5, 40 and $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively.
solution:
Molar masses: HCl = 36.5, CaCO₃ = 100 g/mol
HCl solution: mass = 300 × 1.13 = 339 g
HCl present: 339 × 0.3855 = 130.74 g = 130.74/36.5 = 3.58 mol
CaCO₃: 90/100 = 0.9 mol
Reaction: $CaCO_3 + 2HCl → CaCl_2 + H_2O + CO_2$
HCl required: 0.9 × 2 = 1.8 mol
HCl available: 3.58 mol > 1.8 mol
CaCO₃ is limiting. HCl remaining: (3.58 - 1.8) × 36.5 = 1.78 × 36.5 = 64.97 g

subject: Chemistry
q:
An organic compound weighing 500 mg , produced 220 mg of $\mathrm{CO}_2$. on complete combustion. The percentage composition of carbon in the compound is _____ \%. (nearest integer)
(Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{O}: 16$)
question:
An organic compound weighing 500 mg , produced 220 mg of $\mathrm{CO}_2$. on complete combustion. The percentage composition of carbon in the compound is _____ \%. (nearest integer)
(Given molar mass in $\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ of $\mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{O}: 16$)
solution:
Organic compound $\xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{CuO}} \mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CO}_2}=\frac{220 \times 10^{-3}}{44}=5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{moles} \\ & \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{C}}=5 \times 10^{-3} \times 12\end{aligned}$
$\% \mathrm{~m}$ carbon $=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3} \times 12}{500 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100=12 \%$
Correct answer is $12$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following have same number of significant figures?
(A) 0.00253
(B) 1.0003
(C) 15.0
(D)163
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
question:
Which of the following have same number of significant figures?
(A) 0.00253
(B) 1.0003
(C) 15.0
(D)163
Choose the correct answer from the options given below
solution:
All nonzero digits are significant. All zeros that are found between nonzero digits are significant. Leading zeros (to the left of the first nonzero digit) are not significant. Trailing zeros for a whole number that ends with a decimal point are significant.The number of significant figures of the given numbers are as follows:
Numbers                   No. of significant figures
(A) 0.00253                 3  
(B) 1.0003                   5
(C) 15.0                        3
(D) 163                         3
∴ (A), (C) and (D) have the same number of significant figures.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CNG</mi></math> is an important transportation fuel. When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>gCNG</mi></math> is mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>208</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> oxygen in vehicles, it leads to the formation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> and produces large quantity of heat during this combustion, then the amount of carbon dioxide, produced in grams is [nearest integer] [Assume CNG to be methane]
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>CNG</mi></math> is an important transportation fuel. When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi>gCNG</mi></math> is mixed with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>208</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> oxygen in vehicles, it leads to the formation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> and produces large quantity of heat during this combustion, then the amount of carbon dioxide, produced in grams is [nearest integer] [Assume CNG to be methane]
solution:
CH4+   2O2⟶   CO2+2H2O

10016    20832

Number of molesstoichiometric coefficient for CH4=6.251  Number of molesstoichiometric coefficient for O2=6.52=3.25

The reagent with lower ratio of number of moles to stoichiometric coefficient is called limiting reagent.

Hence, O2 is limiting reagent

Limiting reagent decides the amount of product,

As per the equation 2 moles of O2 gives rise to 1 mole of CO2, So 6.5 moles of O2 gives 6.52 moles of CO2

⇒nCO2=6.52

So mass of CO2=6.52×44=143gm

subject: Chemistry
q: Experimentally it was found that a metal oxide has formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>0.98</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. Metal M, is present as <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> in its oxide. Fraction of the metal which exists as <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> would be:
question: Experimentally it was found that a metal oxide has formula <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>0.98</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math>. Metal M, is present as <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> and <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> in its oxide. Fraction of the metal which exists as <math><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn><mo>+</mo></mrow></msup></math> would be:
solution:
Consider one mole of the oxide, then

Moles of M = 0.98;     Moles of O2- = 1

Let 'x' moles of M are in +3 oxidation state, then Moles of M2+ = 0.98-x

On doing charge balancing we get 

(0.98 - x)2 + 3x  = 2

Or 1.96 - 2x + 3x - 2 = 0

Or x = 0.04

 ∴ %of M3+=0.040.98×100=4.08%

question:

question: Number of moles of methane required to produce <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>22</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msub><mi>CO</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>)</mo></mrow></msub></math> after combustion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></math> moles. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is
solution:
CH4+2O2→CO2+2H2O

Gram molecular mass of CO2=12+2(16)=44 g/mol

From the reaction, it is clear that 1 mole of methane on complete combustion produces 44 g (1 mole) of carbon dioxide.

Therefore moles of CH4 required to produce 22 g of CO2 are:

=144×22=0.5 mol =50×10-2 mol

Hence, m=50

=25 gm

subject: Chemistry
q:
Mass of magnesium required to produce 220 mL of hydrogen gas at STP on reaction with excess of dil. HCl is
Given : Molar mass of Mg is $24 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
question:
Mass of magnesium required to produce 220 mL of hydrogen gas at STP on reaction with excess of dil. HCl is
Given : Molar mass of Mg is $24 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
solution:
$\mathrm{Mg}+2 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow \mathrm{MgCl}_2+\mathrm{H}_2$
Volume $\mathrm{H}_2$ evolved $=220 \mathrm{ml}$
Mole of $\mathrm{H}_2=\frac{220 \times 10^{-3}}{22.4}=$ mole of Mg used
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \text { Mass of Mg used } & =\frac{220 \times 10^{-3}}{22.4} \times 24 \\
& =235.7 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{gm} \\
& =235.7 \mathrm{mg}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Chemistry
q:
A sample of CaCO3 and MgCO3 weighed 2.21g is ignited to constant weight of 1.152g. The composition of the mixture is:

(Given molar mass in g mol−1, CaCO3:100, MgCO3:84)
question:
A sample of CaCO3 and MgCO3 weighed 2.21g is ignited to constant weight of 1.152g. The composition of the mixture is:

(Given molar mass in g mol−1, CaCO3:100, MgCO3:84)
solution:
CaCO3s→ΔCaOs+CO2g

MgCO3s→ΔMgOs+CO2g

Let the weight of CaCO3 be x gm

∴ weight of MgCO3=2.21−x gm

Moles of CaCO3 decomposed = moles of CaO formed

x100= moles of CaO formed

∴ weight of CaO formed =x100×56
Moles of MgCO3 decomposed = moles of MgO formed

2.21−x84= moles of MgO formed

∴  weight of MgO formed =2.21−x84×40 ⇒2.21−x84×40+x100×56=1.152

∴x=1.187g= weight of CaCO3 and weight of MgCO3=1.023g

subject: Chemistry
q: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> nitric acid is heated in a beaker, its volume is reduced to half and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of nitric acid is evaporated. The molarity of the remaining nitric acid solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math>. question: When <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>800</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> nitric acid is heated in a beaker, its volume is reduced to half and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of nitric acid is evaporated. The molarity of the remaining nitric acid solution is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math>. (Molar mass of nitric acid is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>63</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
The number of moles of nitric acid, nHNO3=0.5×0.8

=0.4 mole

nHNO3remains=0.4-11.563

=0.4-0.1825

=0.2175

Molarity =0.2175400×1000

=0.21750.4

=0.5437 mole/lit.

≃0.54 mole/lit.

=54×10-2 mol/lit.

q: The average molar mass of chlorine is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Cl</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>35</mn></mmultiscripts></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Cl</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>37</mn></mmultiscripts></math> in naturally occurring chlorine is close to :
question: The average molar mass of chlorine is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>35</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The ratio of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Cl</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>35</mn></mmultiscripts></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi>Cl</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><none></none><mn>37</mn></mmultiscripts></math> in naturally occurring chlorine is close to :
solution:
35CI37CIMolar ratiox1−x

Mavg.=35×x+371−x=35.5

35x+37−37x=35.5

2x=1.5

x=34

So, ratio of 35CI:37CI=3:1

subject: Chemistry
q:
How many grams of residue is obtained by heating $2.76$ g of silver carbonate?
(Given: Molar mass of C, O and Ag are $12$, $16$ and $108$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively)
question:
How many grams of residue is obtained by heating $2.76$ g of silver carbonate?
(Given: Molar mass of C, O and Ag are $12$, $16$ and $108$ g mol$^{-1}$ respectively)
solution:
Molar mass of $Ag_2CO_3$ = $2 \times 108 + 12 + 3 \times 16 = 276$ g mol$^{-1}$

Number of moles of $Ag_2CO_3$ = $\dfrac{2.76}{276} = 0.01$ mol

The decomposition reaction of silver carbonate on heating is:

$Ag_2CO_3(s) \rightarrow 2Ag(s) + CO_2(g) + \dfrac{1}{2}O_2(g)$

From the stoichiometry of the reaction, $1$ mole of $Ag_2CO_3$ gives $2$ moles of Ag as residue.

Number of moles of Ag formed = $2 \times 0.01 = 0.02$ mol

Mass of Ag residue = $0.02 \times 108 = 2.16$ g

Answer: $2.16$ g
